label: Planning
definition: The capability of an agent to form a sequence of steps toward a goal before or while acting, deciding what to do in what order rather than reacting one move at a time.

body.text: Planning is the part of an agent that decides what to do, in what order, to reach a goal. Rather than choosing each action purely in reaction to the latest observation, a planning agent forms an ordered set of steps that, if carried out, should accomplish the objective. The plan can be drawn up entirely in advance (plan first, then execute), or it can be revised continuously as the agent acts and learns (interleaved planning). Either way, planning is what gives an agent foresight: the ability to look past the immediate next move and arrange a path toward an outcome that no single step would reach.

Planning matters because most worthwhile tasks have structure and dependencies. You cannot run the tests before you have written the code, and you cannot summarize a document you have not yet fetched. An agent that only reacts move by move is prone to wandering, repeating itself, or pursuing dead ends, because it has no representation of where it is headed. An explicit plan gives the agent a backbone to follow, a way to check progress against intended steps, and a place to notice when reality has diverged from expectation and a new plan is needed.

In language-model agents, planning is usually realized in prompts and loops rather than in a separate symbolic planner. A common approach asks the model to first lay out the steps, a form of task-decomposition, and then work through them one at a time. The react-prompting pattern weaves lightweight planning into each turn, where a thought decides the next action in light of the goal and the latest observation. More elaborate schemes let the model explore several candidate plans, score them, and pursue the most promising, and pair planning with reflection so the agent can revise a plan that is not working. Carrying the plan and its progress across steps is the job of agent-memory.

Planning is one of the core competencies that turns a model into a capable ai-agent and is central to any non-trivial agentic-workflow. It is most consequential, and most fragile, in an autonomous-agent, which must plan its own course over a long horizon with little human guidance, where small planning errors can compound across many steps. Strong planning, kept honest by frequent reflection and grounded by real observations from tool-use, is much of what separates an agent that reliably finishes a task from one that drifts.
